topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the overflowing cardboard box in the attic. It was a relic from her childhood, something she hadn't touched since she was ten. Inside, nestled among old photographs and faded linens, were stacks upon stacks of graded papers. Math tests from middle school, essays from high school, even early kindergarten worksheets. Her breath hitched slightly as her fingers brushed against a crumpled history report. "What on earth?" she whispered, the sound swallowed by the dusty silence of the room. She carefully lifted a neatly bound collection of English assignments, the paper crisp despite the age. A strange, unsettled feeling settled in her stomach, a knot tightening beneath her ribs. Her gaze darted around the attic, searching for an explanation, finding only shadows and forgotten things.

[story 2]

"Mom, what is all this?" Leo asked, his voice laced with genuine bewilderment. He was standing in the living room, surveying the chaos of the basement storage closet. There, stacked floor to ceiling, were binders and loose sheets, every single school submission from the past decade. Leo ran a hand through his hair, his brow furrowed deeply. He picked up a geometry homework sheet, turning it over and over. The sheer volume of organized work was staggering. He shook his head slowly, a small, uncertain smile playing on his lips as he tried to reconcile this with the memory of his own organized life. He felt a distinct sense of disorientation, like the ground beneath him had subtly shifted.

[story 3]

I found the box tucked behind the seldom-used winter blankets. My hands trembled slightly as I lifted the lid. It wasn't just old papers; it was an archive. Every assignment, every test, every project my mother had ever completed for me, meticulously filed away. I picked up a stack of chemistry notes, the ink faded but the structure perfectly preserved. I sat down on the dusty floor, the weight of the collection pressing down on me. My jaw went slack for a moment. I leaned back on my heels, staring at the sheer volume, a slow, heavy sigh escaping my lips. I didn't know how to process it; my mind just kept cycling through questions, each one landing with a dull thud.
